narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong start in Year 1, where your grades placed you in the top decile of your school—ranking 9th out of 10, well above the typical student's 6th decile position. However, your Year 3 grades dropped significantly to the 2nd decile, far below the average 6th decile performance across students in the dataset. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 is slightly below the average of 3.21, while your LSAT score of 27 falls well short of the typical 37, and your upper-middle family income aligns with many applicants. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 45% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. The decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 grades standing out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, likely because such a sharp drop signals potential challenges in sustaining performance through law school and beyond. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your excellent Year 1 decile, which pulled strongly in the direction of passing, showing the model recognizes your early promise. Your undergraduate GPA also nudged things positively toward success, though not as powerfully as those top academic rankings. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later-year slide and lower LSAT make your profile riskier, even with your solid start and background. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with upper-middle roots—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your LSAT through prep courses and explaining your Year 3 dip in a future application, perhaps with evidence of recovery like recent strong grades or bar prep plans. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to law school.
